items.myth: Higher entropy always means a better machine learning dataset or model.
items.reality: Higher H(X) means more uncertainty in X itself. For labels, moderate entropy reflects class diversity; maximum entropy is not inherently desirable for prediction—pure nodes (H = 0) are ideal after a split.
items.example: A churn dataset with 50/50 labels has H = 1 bit (high uncertainty). After a perfect split, child nodes have H = 0—that reduction is the goal, not maximizing entropy everywhere.

items.myth: Entropy can be negative for discrete random variables.
items.reality: Shannon entropy of a discrete X is always H(X) ≥ 0, with zero only for deterministic X. Negative values appear in differential entropy for continuous variables, not in standard discrete H(X).
items.example: Fair die: H = log₂ 6 ≈ 2.58 bits > 0. Deterministic X always equal to 4: H = 0.

items.myth: Entropy and variance measure the same kind of spread.
items.reality: Variance depends on numeric distance from the mean. Entropy depends only on probabilities, not label ordering or magnitude—two distributions with identical PMF have the same H regardless of how outcomes are labeled.
items.example: Labels {0, 1} with p = [0.5, 0.5] and labels {100, 1000} with the same p have identical entropy 1 bit but different variance if treated as numeric.

items.myth: Cross-entropy and Shannon entropy are the same quantity.
items.reality: H(X) uses the true PMF p(x) for both weighting and log: −Σ p log p. Cross-entropy H(P, Q) uses p for weighting and q in the log. They coincide only when P = Q.
